nativeRange.habitat: Reef channels, moats, lagoon and seaward reefs with strong surge; depth range 0–180 m

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on coarse leafy brown algae (Sargassum), and has been documented accepting dried nori, spirulina-based flakes, and seaweed preparations in captivity. Supplemental meaty foods such as frozen mysis shrimp are generally accepted and tend to promote good color and health. Feeding at least 3 times weekly is commonly advised.

avoidWith: other Unicorn Tangs (unless in very large tanks)

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke; 3000x2000. Tack-sharp side profile on dark background, blue/purple body sheen and the two vivid blue caudal-peduncle spines clearly visible; horn present (subadult).
Rjcastillo; 7008x3949. Sharp public-aquarium shot with the prominent forehead horn clearly displayed against a blue reef background; body colour is pale under aquarium lighting.
Diego Delso; 5535x3690. Sharp in-situ reef shot showing natural olive coloration, horn nub, and the two bright-blue caudal spines with yellow patches; adds wild-habitat variety.

summary: A large, active surgeonfish from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its prominent forehead horn and vivid blue caudal spines. Generally considered peaceful toward most tankmates but tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics. Best suited to very large aquariums with ample open swimming space and live rock for grazing.
careNotes:
The Bluespine Unicorn Tang is one of the larger surgeonfish kept in the aquarium hobby and generally requires substantial space to thrive. Tank length is generally considered more important than volume given the species' active, linear swimming style.

A characteristic unicorn-like horn develops gradually as the fish grows, typically becoming visible around 6 inches in length and becoming prominent in adults. The caudal peduncle (the narrow wrist of the tail) bears two large, permanently erect spines that are non-retractable and very sharp; care should be taken when handling or netting this species to avoid injury and to avoid entangling the fish in a net.

The Bluespine Unicorn Tang is generally considered reef safe when kept well-fed on a consistent algae-rich diet.

Conspecific aggression is well-documented; this species tends to be aggressive toward others of its kind, particularly in smaller systems. In very large aquariums of 500 gallons or more, pairs or small groups have been kept successfully. The species tends to be peaceful toward unrelated reef fish.

This fish is also sold under the trade name Blue Unicorn Tang, a variation that references the distinctive bluish color of its caudal-peduncle spines; both names refer to the same species.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented for the Bluespine Unicorn Tang. Pair spawning behavior has been observed in the wild, but reproduction has not been achieved under typical aquarium conditions and is not considered a realistic prospect for hobbyists.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (27 in / 68.6 cm), tank size (150 gal / 568 L), temperament, and reef safe status. BlueZoo tank minimum is considerably lower than LiveAquaria's 360-gal recommendation; average applied.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), maximum wild size (70 cm), preferred temperature range (26–29°C, modeled 23.4–28.8°C), max reported age (55 years), native range (Indo-Pacific: Red Sea to Hawaii), and reef-associated habitat description.

additional.name: ReefChasers
additional.notes: Additional tank size data point (250 gal / 946 L); confirmed diet (herbivore with algae focus), temperament (aggressive toward other tangs), and reef safe guidance. Used in tank-size averaging.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented. Pair spawning has been observed in the wild, but reproduction under typical aquarium conditions is not considered feasible.
